name: Al-Mu'izz Street
description: Al-Muizz Street is named after the Fatimid Caliph, al-Muizz li-Din Allah (341–365 AH / 953–975 AD), who first founded this street. He is also the founder of the Fatimid caliphates in Egypt since he ruled Egypt in (358-365AH\ 969-975AD). Today, it is the largest open-air museum for Islamic monuments in the world, and a unique heritage site that was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1979. The street has borne many names over the centuries, and in 1937 it came to be known as al-Muizz in honour of the founder of Cairo.

name: Cairo Citadel
description: The Citadel of Sultan Salah al-Din al-Ayyubi (Saladin) is one of the most iconic monuments in Islamic Cairo, and among the most impressive defensive fortresses dating to the Middle Ages. Its strategic location on the Muqattam Hills gave it a formidable defensive position, and offered, as it still does today, an unrestricted panoramic view of Cairo. Sultan Salah al-Din al-Ayyubi was the first to order the construction of a fortress over the Muqattam Hills in (572 AH/ 1176 AD), but it was not completed during his lifetime. This was achieved during the reign of Sultan al-Kamel ibn al-Adel (604 AH/1207 AD) who decided to reside in it, making it the official residence of the rulers of Egypt.

name: Helwan Corner Museum
description: In 1942, King Farouk built a rest house that came to be known as Farouk’s Corner. It was built in the shape of a sailboat on the bank of the Nile. The royal rest house was then converted into a museum, and in 2016 the Museum was reopened after renovation to the public. The museum displays a collection of objects that once belonged to the royal family including statues, souvenirs, and photographs and oil paintings of members of the royal family.

name: Cairo International Airport Museum - Terminal 2
description: The idea of establishing a museum in Terminal 2 at Cairo International Airport started in 2020. And that is through starting point, through visitors’ episode, on the ancient history of Egypt. Museum was built in the Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities and Ministry of Civil Aviation in an area of about 100 m2 Museum display expresses luminous touches on the history of Egypt throughout its eras. Museum displays unique artifacts that highlight artistic and historical features of each era, starting with ancient Egyptian times, in addition to highlighting characteristics of Roman, Coptic, Islamic, and modern-era arts

name: All Saints’ Cathedral
description: All Saints’ Cathedral is the Anglican (Episcopal) cathedral in Zamalek, built 1988 behind the Cairo Marriott. Designed by Awad and Selim Kamel, it replaced the original 1878 church (demolished for the 6th-Oct Bridge). The modernist concrete church features a lotus-inspired folding roof.

name: The Egyptian Museum
description: The Egyptian Museum is the oldest archaeological museum in the Middle East, and houses the largest collection of Pharaonic antiquities in the world. The museum displays an extensive collection spanning from the Predynastic Period to the Greco-Roman Era. The architect of the building was selected through an international competition in 1895, which was the first of its kind, and was won by the French architect, Marcel Dourgnon. The museum was inaugurated in 1902 by Khedive Abbas Helmy II, and has become a historic landmark in downtown Cairo, and home to some of the world’s most magnificent ancient masterpieces.

name: Museum of Islamic Art
description: The Museum of Islamic Art contains one of the largest and most extensive collections of Islamic artifacts in the world. The idea of collecting and display the grand collection of artifacts began in 1880 AD. Eventually the building was established and inaugurated in 1903 AD, during the region of Khedive Abbas Helmy ll. The building’s facade was made in the Mamluk style and is adjacent to the National Library of Egypt.

name: The Coptic Museum
description: The Coptic Museum houses the largest collection of Coptic artifacts in the world and was inaugurated in 1910. The museum was established through the efforts of Marcus Simaika Pasha, a prominent Coptic figure who was vested in the preservation of Coptic heritage. Simaika Pasha bought and collected Coptic antiquities and various architectural elements from older churches that were undergoing renovation, and used them to built the museum and establish its collection. The collection represents Coptic history from its earliest beginnings in Egypt through to its rise as a leading center of Christianity in the world.

name: National Museum of Egyptian Civilization(NMEC)
description: The NMEC is the first of its kind in Egypt and the Arab world. It presents the full range of the richness and diversity of Egyptian civilization throughout the ages, from prehistoric times to the present day by focusing on its continuity and stability as demonstrated by its tangible and intangible heritage. The museum’s exceptional collection includes the royal mummies, which are exhibited in a new interactive display using 21st century cutting-edge technology to go beneath the wrappings and reveal their secrets, in addition to shedding light on the rituals and religious beliefs surrounding mummification in ancient Egypt.

name: Cairo International Airport Museum - Terminal 3
description: The Cairo International Airport Museum-(Terminal 3) was inaugurated in 2016, in the light of the cooperation protocol between the Ministry of Antiquities and the Ministry of Civil Aviation, to serve as the initial introduction to ancient Egypt, and even the window overlooking its iconic monuments and its majestic civilization. The museum at that time occupied an area of ​​approximately 60 square meters, and 38 objects were displayed in it. In 2020 the museum was transferred from its first location to the fourth floor in the same building, in order to display more objects, as its area occupies about 150 square meters. The museum currently displays a group of the most prominent objects through which the visitor can experience the incomparable history of Egypt through its various, periods.

name: The Royal Carriages Museum
description: The Royal carriages Museum in Boulaq is one of the earliest of its kind worldwide, both from the prospect of the authenticity of its building and the originality of its displays. The building was particularly adapted to preserve the cultural heritage of the royal carriages and all related material dating back to the era of Mohammed Ali Dynasty. The idea of establishing the museum building dates back to the reign of khedive Ismail (r. 1863- 1879), who thought of founding a special structure for housing the horses and the khedivial carriages.

name: Cairo Opera House
description: The main performing arts venue in Cairo, offering opera, ballet, and orchestral performances. It houses multiple theatres and exhibition halls in Opera Square on Gezira Island.

name: Modern Egyptian Art Museum
description: Egypt’s main modern art museum (est. 1927) with ~13,000 works by 20th-century and contemporary Egyptian artists. Located on Opera grounds in Zamalek. Construction began in 1936 on the current structure, designed to complement the surrounding cultural institutions in the Opera House complex.

name: Cairo Tower
description: A 187m tall concrete tower on Gezira Island (built 1961) offering 360° views of Cairo and the Nile. It includes an observation deck and a revolving restaurant.

name: Aisha Fahmy Palace
description: Historic palace (built 1907 in Italianate style) that was home to Princess Aisha Fahmy. Restored as an arts complex with exhibition halls and a café. Charming Nile views.

name: Islamic Ceramics Museum
description: Housed in the Neo-Ottoman Prince Amr Ibrahim Palace (1921), this is Egypt’s first ceramics museum. It showcases historic ceramic art from across the Islamic world.

name: Samiha Kamil Palace
description: Samiha Kamil Palace, built in 1902, was later purchased by Princess Samiha Kamil (daughter of Sultan Hussein Kamil) and converted into a cultural salon. In 1995 it became the Greater Cairo Public Library. The palace’s architecture has Andalusian-inspired style with Moroccan, Ottoman and Renaissance motifs.

name: Zamalek Art Gallery
description: A private gallery promoting contemporary Egyptian artists, hosting rotating exhibitions of modern art and photography. (Gallery space in central Zamalek.)

name: Gayer Anderson Museum (Bayt al-Kritliyya)
description: The Gayer Anderson Museum consists of two houses from the Ottoman period (16th–17th century), the first built by scholar Abd al-Qadir al-Hadad in 1545 AD and the second, known as Bayt al-Kritliyya, dating to 1631 AD. English officer John Gayer Anderson restored and furnished them in Islamic-Arabic style between 1935 and 1942, assembling pharaonic, Islamic, and Asiatic collections that now form one of Cairo's most atmospheric museums, made internationally famous as the setting for the 1977 James Bond film 'The Spy Who Loved Me'.

name: Manial Palace Museum
description: The Manial Palace Museum is one of the most important royal museums in Egypt, representing a defining period of modern Egyptian history. Built by Prince Muhammad Ali Tawfik between 1900 and 1929 on Rhoda Island, the palace blends Islamic, Persian, Mamluk, Syrian, Moroccan, Andalusian, and Ottoman architectural styles within a single complex. It includes a reception hall, clock tower, sabil, mosque, hunting museum, throne hall, private museum, and golden hall, all surrounded by magnificent gardens.

name: Bab Zuwayla
description: Bab Zuwayla is the last surviving southern gate of Fatimid Cairo, built in 1092 AD by Vizier Badr al-Jamali during the reign of Caliph al-Mustansir. Named after the Zuwayla tribe from North Africa, it was also called Bawabat al-Mitwalli. The gate consists of two semi-circular towers with the entrance between them; its towers were later used by Sultan al-Mu'ayyad as minarets for his adjacent mosque. Bab Zuwayla witnessed the hanging of the last Mamluk sultan Tumanbay by the Ottoman Sultan Selim I in 1517 AD.

name: Bab al-Futuh
description: Bab al-Futuh (Gate of Conquests) is one of the northern gates of Fatimid Cairo's walls, built by Vizier Badr al-Jamali in 1087 AD during the reign of Caliph al-Mustansir. Together with Bab al-Nasr and Bab Zuwayla, it is among the finest examples of pre-Crusade Islamic military architecture. Its two rounded towers are linked by a platform with shafts for pouring boiling oil on invaders. The arch features a lattice of diamonds with sculpted shapes, and beautifully decorated corbel stones crown the entrance.

name: Bab al-Nasr
description: Bab al-Nasr (Gate of Victory) is one of the two northern gates of Fatimid Cairo's walls, built in 1087 AD alongside Bab al-Futuh by Vizier Badr al-Jamali during the reign of Caliph al-Mustansir. Unlike the rounded towers of Bab al-Futuh, Bab al-Nasr has two square towers with the entrance between them. A series of shields carved in raised relief adorns the facade, representing the protective function of the Fatimid fortifications as the shields of the city.

name: Bayt al-Suhaymi
description: Bayt al-Suhaymi is one of the finest examples of historic domestic architecture in Cairo, located in the Darb al-Asfar lane on Al-Mu'izz Street. It combines two Ottoman houses merged into one: the first built in 1648 AD by Sheikh Abd al-Wahhab al-Tablawi, and the second built in 1796 AD by Haji Ismail Chelebi. Named after the last resident, Sheikh Muhammad Amin al-Suhaymi, it features magnificent mashrabiyya (wooden lattice) windows, a large loggia (maq'ad), furnished reception halls, a birthing chair, and a working antique waterwheel.

name: Mosque of al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah
description: The Mosque of al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah is the fourth oldest mosque in Egypt and the second largest after the Mosque of Ibn Tulun. Its construction was begun by Caliph al-Aziz bi-Allah in 990 AD and completed by his son al-Hakim in 1013 AD, near Bab al-Futuh at the end of Al-Mu'izz Street. The mosque served as a Shiite religious center during the Fatimid period. Its grand projecting entrance, one of the oldest architectural examples of this form in Egypt, was influenced by the Great Mosque of Mahdiya in Tunisia.

name: Madrasa, Mausoleum and Bimaristan of al-Mansur Qalawun
description: The complex of Sultan Sayf al-Din Qalawun, built in 1283–1284 AD on Al-Mu'izz Street, is one of the finest Mamluk architectural achievements in Egypt. It comprises a mosque, a school (madrasa), a mausoleum, and a hospital (bimaristan) — the world's first purpose-built psychiatric hospital. Its entrances are characterized by unique designs with gold and silver gilding, geometric and floral motifs. The hospital originally treated people with nervous and psychological disorders and today operates as an eye and dental hospital.

name: Mosque and Madrasa of al-Nasir Muhammad Ibn Qalawun
description: Located on Al-Mu'izz Street between the two palaces, this madrasa was begun during the reign of Sultan Katbugha in 1296 AD and completed by Sultan Muhammad ibn Qalawun between 1298 and 1303 AD. It is renowned for its unique portal, taken from a Gothic-style Crusader church in Acre (modern-day Israel) and incorporated into the Mamluk entrance, and its minaret decorated with colored faience tiles from Persia — a rare feature in Egyptian Islamic architecture.

name: Sultan al-Ghuri Complex
description: The Sultan al-Ghuri Complex, built between 1503 and 1504 AD by Sultan Qansuh al-Ghuri, one of the last Circassian Mamluk sultans, is one of the most beautiful monuments in Cairo. It spans both sides of Al-Mu'izz Street, connected by a wooden roof spanning the street and sheltering a textiles market. The complex includes a mosque-madrasa, mausoleum, khanqah, sabil, kuttab, dormitory, and wikala. Notable features include the madrasa's square minaret crowned by four pinnacles — the first in Cairo — and its ivory-and-ebony inlaid wooden minbar.

name: Mosque of al-Zahir Baybars
description: One of the oldest Mamluk mosques, built in 1266 AD by Sultan Baybars al-Bunduqdari on Al-Zahir Square, spanning approximately 12,600 square meters. Four arcades surround a central courtyard; the qibla riwaq was originally covered by a massive wooden dome. The mosque suffered greatly over centuries — used as a military fort by the French campaign (1798–1801), its minaret demolished; then used as a British military warehouse and slaughterhouse. Fully restored in a cooperative project between Egypt and Kazakhstan completed in 2022.

name: Palace of Prince Taz
description: The Palace of Prince Taz on Al-Suyufiyya Street in the Al-Khalifa neighborhood was commissioned by Prince Saif al-Din Taz, cupbearer and deputy of Aleppo, in 1352 AD. It is one of the finest and most spacious Bahari Mamluk palace examples, characterized by its large maq'ad (loggia) overlooking the inner courtyard and elaborately decorated halls. The ceilings feature geometric and plant motifs; walls bear inscriptions with the prince's titles and his emblem, the cup, indicating his role. The palace is currently free to enter.

name: House of Gamal al-Din al-Dhahabi
description: The House of Gamal al-Din al-Dhahabi, attributed to the Shah Bandar al-Tujjar (chief merchant) of Cairo, was built in 1637 AD in the Khashqadam Lane of the al-Ghuriyya neighborhood. It reflects the development of Ottoman residential architecture in Egypt. The house has three stone facades with a ground floor of storerooms and shops, a courtyard with a central fountain, and two upper floors dividing the salamlek (men's quarters) containing a maq'ad, bathroom, and upper hall, from the haramlek (women's quarters).

name: House of Sitt Wasila
description: The House of Sitt Wasila, a remarkable example of Ottoman domestic architecture, was established in 1664 AD by brothers Abd al-Haqq and Lutfi al-Kinani. Named after its last resident, Lady Wasila Khatun, the house has three levels including a maq'ad with gilded wooden ceiling bearing inscriptions of Ayat al-Kursi, scenic drawings of the holy precincts of Mecca and Medina, a haramlek, and a hammam. Restored in 2005, it now serves as the headquarters of the 'House of Arab Poetry', hosting cultural activities.

name: Sabil-Kuttab of Muhammad Ali
description: Muhammad Ali Pasha built this sabil-kuttab in 1820 AD as a charitable offering for his son Ahmad Tusun Pasha, who died in 1816 AD. Following the round-fronted Ottoman sabil type, it has five metal-latticed windows above marble basins. The facade is lavishly covered with white marble decorated with floral and geometric patterns and Turkish inscriptions. The wooden projecting eave features geometric and floral motifs reflecting Ottoman Baroque and Rococo styles.

name: Mosque of al-Fakahani
description: Originally built by Fatimid Caliph al-Zafir in 1148 AD and known as al-Afkhar or al-Zafir Mosque, this building was later called al-Fakahani after a merchant who restored it. The original Fatimid building's beautiful wooden doors are all that survive of the original structure. The mosque was rebuilt in 1735 AD by Prince Ahmad Katkhuda Mustahfizan al-Kharbutli. Built over a series of shops, it became a 'suspended mosque'.

name: Sabil of al-Amir Shaykhu
description: The Sabil of al-Amir Shaykhu, built in 1354 AD by Prince Sayf al-Din Shaykhu al-Nasiri, one of the Mamluks of al-Nasir Muhammad ibn Qalawun, is one of the oldest standalone sabils not attached to another facility. It is entirely carved into the rock except for its stone-covered facade, making it a unique cave-like structure. Its magnificent facade features a prominent rectangular block with a central entrance topped by a knotted arch decorated with five arches carrying vegetal motifs, inscriptions, and a cupbearer's rank in the shape of a cup.

name: Imam al-Shafi'i Mosque
description: The Mosque of Imam al-Shafi'i in the Southern Cemetery of Cairo is dedicated to Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i, founder of the Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence, who died in Cairo in 820 AD. The current structure was built by Abd al-Rahman Katkhuda in 1762 AD and later renovated by Khedive Tawfiq, with completion by Khedive Abbas Helmi II. The mosque features a Mamluk-style minaret, a wooden minbar inlaid with ivory and ebony, and a mausoleum containing the tomb of Imam al-Shafi'i in a magnificent 13th-century cedar-wood cenotaph.

name: Mosque, Khanqa, and Madrasa of Sultan Farag ibn Barquq
description: Located in the Eastern Mamluk Cemetery (the City of the Dead), this complex was built by Sultan al-Nasir Faraj ibn Barquq between 1398 and 1411 AD and is one of the largest architectural units in Egypt. It comprises a mosque, a khanqah for Sufis, and a madrasa, and serves as the burial place of Sultan Barquq and his family. The complex is dominated by two matching minarets and two large domes flanking the qibla facade. Two sabils adorn the main facade.

name: Baron Empain Palace
description: Baron Empain Palace in Heliopolis (Masr al-Gedida) is one of Cairo's most distinctive landmarks, built between 1907 and 1911 by Belgian industrialist Baron Edward Empain. The palace was designed by French architect Alexander Marcel, inspired by Cambodian Hindu temples, featuring a distinctive tower, rotating top floor (now inactive), and elaborate stonework blending Hindu, Buddhist, and European architectural details. The palace is surrounded by a garden on all sides and includes a basement, ground floor, and two upper floors. Restored and opened to the public in 2020.

name: Maimonides Synagogue
description: The Maimonides Synagogue in Old Cairo is attributed to Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides / Rambam), the great Cordoban-born Jewish philosopher and physician born in 1135 AD, who settled in Egypt and died in Cairo in 1204 AD. Built at the site where Maimonides lived, the synagogue includes a main prayer hall with a marble bimah platform, a Torah cabinet, a women's section, a Yeshiva (teaching room), and the well used for purification. Restored between 2007 and 2010 by the Supreme Council of Antiquities.

name: Mary's Tree (Matariyya)
description: Mary's Tree in Matariyya marks a sacred site on the Holy Family's flight route into Egypt, where the Virgin Mary and infant Jesus are said to have rested in the shade of a tree. The original tree eventually fell in 1656 AD after centuries, and Franciscan priests gathered its branches and replanted them. The site attracted medieval Arab historians al-Maqrizi and Imam al-Suyuti, European Orientalist travelers, and the French Empress Eugenie during the 1869 Suez Canal inauguration. The current tree with its ancient well and related Coptic chapel is a venerated pilgrimage site.

name: Obelisk of Senwosret I (Ayn Shams / Ancient Heliopolis)
description: The Obelisk of Senwosret I in Ayn Shams (ancient Heliopolis / Iunu) is the oldest standing obelisk in Egypt and one of the oldest in the world, dating to the 12th Dynasty (c. 1956–1911 BC). It stands at the site of ancient Heliopolis, once the most important religious center in Egypt, dedicated to the sun god Ra-Atum, and likely as large as Karnak Temple. The obelisk is a sun ray represented in stone and remains the only significant above-ground trace of this once-great city, which was largely demolished for medieval Cairo's construction.

name: Al-Fustat (Old Cairo Archaeological Site)
description: Al-Fustat, Egypt's first Islamic capital, was founded in 641 AD by General Amr ibn al-As after the Arab conquest of Egypt. Named after the Arabic word for 'tent', it grew into a major city containing the Mosque of Amr ibn al-As (the oldest mosque in Africa), the Ben Ezra Synagogue, numerous ancient Coptic churches, the Nilometer on Rhoda Island, and excavated ruins of the original urban fabric. Today it forms the heart of Old Cairo, a UNESCO-associated site containing some of the oldest continuously inhabited urban space in the country.
